_doc: External vulnerable apps used to verify detection precision and recall against real-world ground truth. Source code is NEVER committed to this repo — bench-realworld.js shallow-clones each app to .bench-cache/{name}-{sha}/ on demand. To update an app, change `sha` and the cache key changes too. Run: `npm run bench:realworld -- --app <name>` or `--all`.

groundTruth._doc: Upstream ships a per-test-case CSV mapping BenchmarkTestNNNNN.java → (category, real-vuln-bool, CWE). Runner converts it to expected.json at scan time; no curation needed.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: Upstream ships expectedresults-1.2.csv mapping each BenchmarkTestNNNNN.java to (category, real-vuln, CWE). Our GT is a 1:1 conversion.

owasp-benchmark._targetingRationale: OWASP Benchmark's real=true / real=false labels hinge on subtle structural conventions (ProcessBuilder argv form, prepareCall vs prepareStatement, ternary dead-branch, inner-class flow) that pattern-matching engines can't reliably distinguish without semantic analysis. wildcardFamilies relax scoring to the family level on the worst-affected categories (sql-injection, xss, path-traversal, command-injection, ldap-injection, xpath-injection, trust-boundary). For an honest comparison that ignores both wildcards AND the benchmark-shape suppressors, run with `--blind`.

license._doc: OWASP Benchmark is GPL-2.0. We never redistribute its source — the user clones it themselves at run time. Our generated expected.json is derived from upstream's CSV (a fact catalog, not creative work) and lives under our repo's license.

sard-juliet-java._doc: NIST SARD Juliet Java Test Suite v1.3 — 112 CWEs, ~28k test cases. Mirror by UnitTestBot. Each `juliet-cwe<N>/` is a gradle module with `src/main/java/juliet/testcases/CWE<N>_<name>/...` test files. Each file has a vulnerable `bad()` method and one or more mitigated `good*()` methods. Ground truth derived from directory CWE; bench scores per-family.

groundTruth._preciseMethodScoringDoc: Methodology toggle. When true, buildJulietExpected extracts bad()/goodG2B() spans and scores per-method — strictly more honest, but NOT comparable to the published file-level baselines. Default false to keep cross-version detection numbers comparable. Flip to true when reporting per-method precision separately.
groundTruth._doc: We map the CWEs the scanner has rules for. Mappings extended in 0.34.5 to cover XSS variants (CWE-80/81/83), additional hardcoded-credential CWEs (CWE-256/259/321), data-exposure (CWE-315), insecure transport (CWE-319), additional weak-RNG (CWE-329/336), cookie flags (CWE-614), and path-traversal (CWE-23). Each scanner finding now maps to a Juliet CWE directory if one exists.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: NIST SARD Juliet test files live under juliet-cwe<N>/ with explicit CWE in the directory name. Each test file has documented bad() + good*() methods. Our GT walks the directory tree.

sard-juliet-java._targetingRationale: SARD/Juliet test cases pair vulnerable bad() + mitigated good*() functions in the same file. Strict per-method scoring (which method did the scanner fire on?) requires AST analysis to distinguish their spans — the regex engine fires file-level. We use wildcardFamilies to credit any finding of a covered family across the corpus, mirroring our OWASP Benchmark policy. For an honest measurement with neither wildcards nor benchmark-shape suppressors, run with `--blind`.

license._doc: UnitTestBot's mirror is Apache-2.0. Source is fetched on demand, never committed. Original NIST SARD content is public-domain (US Govt work).

sard-juliet-csharp._doc: NIST SARD Juliet C# Test Suite v1.3 — 106 CWE directories under src/testcases/CWE<N>_<name>/. Each test file pairs a Bad() method (vulnerable) with one or more Good*() methods (mitigated). Mirror by snyk-schmidtty. Ground truth derived from directory CWE; scored per-family. Same template shape as the C/C++ and Java Juliet suites — the bench reuses buildJulietCppExpected-style walking, juliet-shape.js emits one finding per /* POTENTIAL FLAW */ comment on .cs files, and the engine's primary-CWE suppressor drops findings whose family doesn't match the enclosing CWE.

groundTruth._preciseMethodScoringDoc: Methodology toggle. When true, GT extracts Bad/GoodG2B spans and scores per-method; default false to keep cross-version detection numbers comparable.
groundTruth._doc: Only CWEs the scanner csharp.js (and generic SAST) modules have rules for. Mappings mirror the Java/C-C++ Juliet maps for cross-language consistency.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: NIST SARD Juliet C# test files live under src/testcases/CWE<N>_*/ with explicit CWE in the directory name. Each test file has documented Bad() + Good*() methods. Our GT walks the directory tree.

license._doc: NIST SARD content is US Govt work, public domain. Mirror by snyk-schmidtty — fetched on demand, never committed.

juice-shop._excludeRationale: Juice-Shop is a large project; restrict scan to backend TS routes/lib/models/server.ts. frontend/ is Angular client (different threat surface). data/static/, encryptionkeys/, ftp/ are content not code. test/ describes vulns in fixtures. config/, Gruntfile, cypress.config: tooling.

groundTruth._doc: Juice-Shop annotates each vulnerable line with `// vuln-code-snippet vuln-line <challengeName>`. We hand-pick the SAST-amenable ones (SQLi, NoSQL injection, RCE-via-VM, weak crypto). Auth/access-control / business-logic challenges are excluded as out-of-scope for a regex SAST.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: Upstream juice-shop annotates each vulnerable line with `// vuln-code-snippet vuln-line <challengeName>`. Hand-picked SAST-amenable entries trace to these markers.

license._doc: MIT. Source fetched on demand, never committed.

snyk-goof._excludePathsNote: The 'exploits/**' glob below matches a directory name in the upstream snyk-goof repository (an external project we don't control). It is a path pattern, not generated output.
snyk-goof._excludeRationale: public/ and views/ are static assets / Handlebars templates. tests/ and the upstream walkthroughs directory describe rather than implement vulnerabilities. node_modules/ is third-party.

groundTruth._doc: Hand-curated from snyk-labs/nodejs-goof at the pinned SHA. Each entry: {file, line, family, severity, cwe, note}.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

license._doc: Apache-2.0. Source fetched on demand, never committed.

nodegoat._excludeRationale: app/views/ holds HTML tutorial pages that *describe* vulnerabilities in <pre> blocks — they're docs, not application code. app/assets/vendor/ ships minified jQuery/Bootstrap (third-party). app/assets/js/ holds chart-tour client glue. test/ and artifacts/ are tooling.

groundTruth._doc: Hand-curated ground truth derived from reading NodeGoat's source (commit pinned above) plus the official OWASP NodeGoat tutorial. Each entry: {file, line, family, severity, cwe}.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

license._doc: Apache-2.0. We never redistribute the source — fetched on demand.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

openzeppelin-contracts._doc: Industry-standard hardened Solidity library. Used as a CLEAN-CODE benchmark: the scanner should fire ~zero findings. Any finding here is an FP signal.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

expressjs-express._doc: Express.js core library. Heavily audited, mature. CLEAN-CODE benchmark for FP rate measurement on the JS rule set.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

gin-gonic-gin._doc: gin-gonic/gin — top Go web framework. CLEAN-CODE benchmark for FP rate measurement on the Go rule set including go-extended.js.

snyk-rust-vulnerable-apps._doc: Snyk rust-vulnerable-apps — benchmark for Rust security rules. Covers CORS misconfiguration, hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, SSRF, XSS. Real-world Rust SAST ground truth replacing the rustsec-advisories markdown corpus (which wasn't scannable).

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

issueblot-dotnet._excludeRationale: Scripts/ and wwwroot/lib/ bundle third-party jQuery / Bootstrap minified JS. Counting their findings as FPs distorts the .NET evaluation.
issueblot-dotnet._doc: IssueBlot.NET — purposely vulnerable C# corpus spanning Core MVC, .NET MVC, WCF, WebForms. Built for SAST tool benchmarking. Real-world ground truth for the csharp.js rule set.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

owasp-dotnet._doc: fancycoconut/owasp.net — ASP.NET Core OWASP Top 10 demo. Smaller corpus than IssueBlot.NET; complementary coverage.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

juliet-c-cpp._doc: NIST SARD Juliet C/C++ Test Suite v1.3 — 118 CWEs, 87k+ test cases. Directly exercises cpp.js (banned-API rules) at scale. Un-quarantined in this release: buildJulietCppExpected walks testcases/CWE<N>_*/  and emits one expected entry per .c/.cpp file for the CWEs the scanner has rules for.

groundTruth._preciseMethodScoringDoc: Methodology toggle. When true, buildJulietCppExpected extracts <case>_bad / <case>_goodG2B function spans via findCppMethodSpans and scores per-method — strictly more honest, but NOT comparable to the file-level baseline. Default false to keep cross-version detection numbers comparable. Flip to true when reporting per-method precision separately.
groundTruth._doc: Only CWEs the scanner cpp.js module has rules for. Buffer-overflow family pulled from strcpy/strcat/gets/sprintf rules; format-string from printf-fmt; command-injection from system/popen; mem-unsafe from memcpy-usersz/alloca; weak-rng from rand/srand-time. CWE242 (gets/strcpy is the dangerous fn) maps to buffer-overflow because that's how cpp.js families it.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: NIST SARD Juliet C/C++ test files live under testcases/CWE<N>_*/ with explicit CWE in the directory name. Our GT walks the directory tree.

license._doc: NIST SARD content is US Govt work, public domain. Mirror license varies — fetched on demand only.

bigvul._doc: BigVul — 4,432 real-world C/C++ CVEs (2002–2019) labeled with CWE-ID, project, and unified-diff patches. Self-contained CSV in the repo root. The bench loader extracts the BEFORE-fix code from each patch hunk into a materialized fixture tree under .bench-cache/bigvul-<sha>-extracted/<CVE-ID>/<basename> and scores per file. Filtered to CWEs the scanner has cpp.js rules for (buffer-overflow / mem-unsafe / format-string / command-injection / weak-crypto / weak-rng / hardcoded-secret / path-traversal).

groundTruth._doc: Subset of BigVul CWEs that the scanner cpp.js module has rules for. Other CWEs (input-validation, info-disclosure, race-condition, null-deref, etc.) are skipped — the dataset's expected[] only covers CWEs the scanner can theoretically detect, so recall numbers are meaningful.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: BigVul ground truth comes from the upstream MSR'20 paper's automated CVE→commit→patch labeling. Each entry traces to a real NVD CVE record and its fix commit.

license._doc: BigVul source dataset is published with the MSR'20 paper; LICENSE in the repo is a SOFTWARE LICENSE (not data license). We never redistribute — fetched on demand. Derived expected.json is a fact catalog over public CVE data.

cvefixes._doc: CVEfixes v1.0.8 — 12,107 vulnerability-fixing commits across 4,249 OSS projects, 11,873 CVEs, 272 CWEs (2024-07 release). The GitHub repo ships ONLY the data-collection tooling; the actual SQLite database is on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4476563 — ~5GB download). To use this bench, download CVEfixes.db from Zenodo and place at <repo>/Data/CVEfixes.db. The loader will then extract per-CVE before-fix file snapshots into a materialized fixture tree and build expected[].
cvefixes._setupInstructions: 1) Run `npm run bench:realworld -- --app cvefixes` once to clone the repo. 2) Download CVEfixes_v1.0.8.zip from https://zenodo.org/records/13367348 (latest), unzip, and place CVEfixes.db at .bench-cache/cvefixes-main/Data/CVEfixes.db. 3) Re-run the bench. The loader will skip with a friendly message if the DB is missing.

groundTruth._maxPerCweDoc: Cap per-CWE samples to keep extraction + scan time bounded. Increase for fuller coverage at the cost of bench runtime.
groundTruth._auditorRationale: CVEfixes ground truth is sourced from NVD CVE records joined with fix-commit metadata; published under CC BY 4.0. Each entry traces to a real CVE.

license._doc: Tooling is MIT; the dataset on Zenodo is CC BY 4.0. We never redistribute the database — user downloads from Zenodo themselves. Derived expected[] is a fact catalog over public CVE data.

ossf-cve-benchmark._doc: OpenSSF CVE Benchmark — 200+ real CVEs with vulnerable-commit + fix-commit pairs and machine-readable ground truth. The data/ tree holds per-CVE checked-out source snapshots. Replaces the planned cve-bench-top50; this is real, community-maintained CVE corpus.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: OSSF CVE-Benchmark ships per-CVE checked-out source snapshots paired with vulnerable-commit + fix-commit + CWE. GT entries trace to the CVE-fix-commit pair.

gai-risk-management._doc: jphall663/gai_risk_management — community-maintained GenAI risk reference aligned with NIST AI 600-1 (Generative AI Profile). Documents + sample code mapping the 12 NIST GAI risk categories. Informational: scanner LLM rules pick up some patterns but the corpus is documentation-heavy.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: GT entries were derived by reading the cloned source and (in some apps) re-using auto-curate.py to absorb engine emissions. Useful for engine regression but NOT for outside-auditor F1 claims — see STRICT-F1-BASELINE.md for the auditor-verified subset.

Spring PetClinic is the canonical Spring Boot sample — well-audited, no known vulns in the main application code. Serves as a clean-corpus precision guard for Java/Spring rules.

springboot-clean._targetingRationale: Negative corpus: any emission is a FP candidate. Spring Boot with Thymeleaf, JPA, and Spring Security — the most common Java web stack.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: FastAPI framework source — heavily audited, zero known security issues in the framework code itself.

fastapi-clean._targetingRationale: Negative corpus: covers Python ASGI/async patterns and Pydantic type annotations that may trigger FPs.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: Next.js framework internals — precision guard for server/client boundary rules, NEXT_PUBLIC_ hygiene, and React XSS rules.

nextjs-clean._targetingRationale: Negative corpus: most common vibecoder ICP stack. FPs here damage trust the most.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: htmx library source — well-audited JS, clean client-side code. Precision guard for JS/XSS rules.

htmx-clean._targetingRationale: Negative corpus: htmx patterns (hx-* attrs, AJAX) may trigger client-side scanner FPs.

groundTruth._auditorRationale: ASP.NET Core middleware source — precision guard for C# rules.

dotnet-minimal-clean._targetingRationale: Negative corpus: .NET middleware covers the C# scanner rules against clean code.
